contentHtml:
The Practice of Resource Building for Intimacy

The orgasm ended. The loneliness didn’t.

This is the quiet horror so many of you live in. You can perform connection, you can go through the motions of intimacy, you can even find a moment of physical release, but when the echoes of it fade, you are left in a profound and terrifying emptiness. You reach for your partner, and it feels like you are grasping at smoke. You are touched, and it feels like a violation because there is nothing inside you to receive it. This isn’t a failure of love. It is a failure of resource. You cannot give what you do not have. You cannot meet another in the depths when you are living in the shallows of your own being, utterly depleted.

What Do We Mean By "Resource"?

Let’s be clear. When I speak of resource, I am not talking about the flimsy currencies of the profane world. I am not talking about how much money is in your bank account, how many hours you have free in your day, or how much social status you have accumulated. These things are ghosts. They cannot fill you. They cannot hold you in the dead of night when your own skin feels like a foreign country. I've served thousands of seekers, healers, teachers, leaders, and couples. I guide toward transparency, authenticity, self-awareness, and emotional growth. And in all that work, the fundamental poverty I see is not material, but internal. It is a poverty of presence.

Resource, in the territory of conscious intimacy, is the felt, embodied capacity to be with what is. It is the charge in your nervous system that allows you to stay present when you are activated, instead of checking out, shutting down, or blowing up. It is the wellspring of vitality in your cells, the deep, resonant hum of your own life force. It is the ability to feel your own feet on the ground when the world is spinning, to feel the column of your own spine, to inhabit your own flesh as a sanctuary, not a prison. Without this internal resource, intimacy is not possible. What you call intimacy is merely a transaction—an exchange of need, a negotiation of trauma, a desperate pantomime of connection.

The Great Depletion

Why are we so empty? You live in a world designed to drain you, to pull your life force out through a thousand tiny straws. Your attention is the most valuable commodity on earth, and every corporation, every algorithm, every headline is engineered to harvest it. You are taught to live from the neck up, a disembodied consciousness floating in a sea of digital noise. Your body becomes a machine to be optimized, a problem to be solved, an object to be disciplined, rather than the sacred ground of your own existence.

In this state of constant distraction and disembodiment, you develop coping mechanisms that masquerade as wisdom. She gets just fluent enough in her own damage that she starts sounding wise. But really, she's just a few pages ahead of the kid in the back of the bus. You learn the language of therapy, the jargon of wellness, the postures of spirituality. You talk a good game about your boundaries, your needs, your trauma. But it is all a head-trip. It has not landed in the body. It is a sophisticated form of avoidance. And then you encounter the absolute fraud of the modern spiritual marketplace. You are told to just think positive, to ignore the gaping wound in your chest. This is a brutal violence. It is toxic positivity — 'Everything happens for a reason!' shouted while you're bleeding internally. It is a demand that you abandon the truth of your own experience for a cheap and easy lie.

Comfort is not the same as healing.

Your Body, The First and Final Temple

The journey back to resource begins and ends in one place: the body. Not the idea of the body, but the living, breathing, aching, trembling, sweating reality of it. For decades, I have watched people try to meditate their way out of their trauma, to affirm their way out of their loneliness, to visualize their way out of their emptiness. It does not work. You cannot bypass the body. The body is where the bill for all your unprocessed life comes due. It is where the grief you swallowed, the rage you suppressed, and the terror you ignored have taken up residence. It is the only honest thing in the room.

The classical Tantra that informs my work is not about exotic sexual positions or chasing some mystical state of bliss. It is about turning toward the raw, unfiltered reality of your own embodied experience with unwavering presence. It is the practice of meeting sensation—all sensation—as a direct expression of the divine. The tingling in your hands, the tightness in your jaw, the flutter in your belly, the ache in your heart… this is the scripture of the body. Learning to read it, to be with it, to allow it to move and change without judgment, is the first step in building real, sustainable resource. Your body is not a problem to be fixed. It is the very ground of your being, the temple in which all healing, all connection, all love, becomes possible.

The Foundational Practice: Inhabiting Your Breath

If the body is the temple, the breath is the high priestess. It is the most direct, immediate, and powerful tool you have for shifting your state and building resource. Most of you have forgotten how to breathe. You breathe in shallow sips, high in the chest, a constant state of low-grade panic. This kind of breathing tells your nervous system that you are under threat, that you must be ready to fight or flee. You cannot connect from this place. You can only survive.

The practice is devastatingly simple. And you will resist it with every fiber of your being because it is so potent. Stop what you are doing right now. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel the weight of your body on the chair. And bring your attention to your breath. Don’t change it. Just notice it. Where do you feel it? In your nostrils? In your chest? In your belly? Now, gently, invite the breath to deepen. Imagine it dropping down, down, down into the bowl of your pelvis. Let your belly soften and expand on the inhale. Let it gently fall on the exhale. Do this for three minutes. That’s it.

What you are doing is sending a powerful signal to your nervous system: you are safe. You are here. You are alive. From this place of grounded presence, everything changes. A client once described this to me as feeling like he was "coming home to himself for the first time." That is resource. It is the feeling of being at home in your own skin. This isn’t a one-time fix. It is a practice. It is something you return to, again and again, a hundred times a day. It is the foundational act of filling your own well.

Your trauma is not your identity. Stop wearing it like a badge.

Alan Watts's Guesthouse: Welcoming the Messengers

As you begin to build resource through breath and embodiment, you will start to feel more. This is not always pleasant. The numbness begins to thaw, and what lies beneath it is often a chaotic jumble of unprocessed emotion. The poet Alan Watts speaks of this in his poem "The Guesthouse," where he invites us to welcome every emotion—joy, depression, meanness—as an honored guest, a "guide from beyond." This is a radical act in a world that teaches you to pathologize or get rid of any feeling that is uncomfortable.

Building resource means expanding your capacity to host these messengers. It means when a wave of sadness washes over you, you don’t immediately distract yourself or try to fix it. You breathe into it. You feel where it lives in your body. You allow it to be there, without making it wrong. You are not the sadness. You are the vast, open space in which the sadness can arise and pass. This practice of allowing, of non-resistance, is what builds true emotional resilience. It is what allows you to be a safe harbor for yourself, and therefore, for another. When you are no longer terrified of your own inner world, you are no longer terrified of the messiness of real intimacy. You can meet your partner in their joy, their rage, their grief, because you have learned how to meet yourself in yours.

From Internal Resource to Intimate Resonance

Here, the magic happens. When you have cultivated a deep well of internal resource, your entire way of relating to others transforms. You stop coming to your partner as a beggar, with an empty cup, demanding that they fill you. You come as a sovereign being, with a cup that is already overflowing. You are no longer seeking a savior; you are seeking a fellow god to dance with. Your presence becomes a gift. Your touch becomes a transmission of the vitality you have cultivated in yourself.

This is what it means to love from a place of abundance rather than scarcity. It is the difference between a transaction and a sacred union. When two people who have done the work of building their own resource come together, they create a third field, a field of resonance. In this field, you don’t lose yourself. You become more yourself. You are seen, not for the persona you project, but for the deep, luminous truth of your being. This is the promise of conscious intimacy. It is not about finding the right person. It is about becoming the right person. It is about doing the deep, often uncomfortable work of building the internal resource to be able to meet another in love, truth, and freedom. Loving like a wild god means loving fiercely, without restraint. It means standing in front of someone and saying, 'I get you, but I also get the truth.'

The Courage to Be a Sanctuary

The final stage of this practice is recognizing that your own regulated, resourced nervous system is the greatest gift you can offer to your partner, your family, and the world. To be in the presence of someone who is truly at home in their own body is a healing experience. It is a transmission. It tells the nervous system of everyone around them, "You can rest now. You are safe." This is not a passive state. It is an active stance in the world. It is the courage to be a sanctuary in a world of chaos.

Your Instagram spirituality is showing.

This requires you to be fiercely protective of your own well-being. It means saying no. It means disappointing people. It means choosing the silence of the forest over the noise of the party. It means prioritizing your own practice, your own embodiment, above all else. It is the most generous act you can perform. From this place of profound self-possession, you can finally offer the quality of presence that true intimacy demands. You can listen without needing to fix. You can touch without needing to take. You can love without needing to be saved. You become a haven, not because you are perfect, but because you are present. You have done the work to fill your own well, and now you have an infinite supply to offer.

The Lie of Self-Improvement

Let's dismantle another insidious lie: the cult of self-improvement. This is the idea that you are a problem to be solved, a project to be completed. You are constantly striving, fixing, optimizing, and you are always falling short. This is not a path to wholeness. It is a path to perpetual self-loathing. It is another form of depletion, another way of abandoning the truth of who you are right now for some fantasy of who you think you should be. The work of building resource is not about adding anything to yourself. It is about subtracting. It is about letting go of the layers of conditioning, the false beliefs, the armor you have built around your heart. It is a process of returning to the magnificent, messy, and utterly divine being that you already are.

The truth is, the capacity for the depth of intimacy you crave is already within you. It is not something you need to acquire; it is something you need to uncover. It is buried under years of conditioning, of distraction, of ignoring the wisdom of your own body. The work of resource building is the work of excavation. It is the practice of returning, again and again, to the sacred ground of your own being. The question isn't whether you're ready. It's whether you're willing. Stop reading. Go feel your feet on the earth.

The information on this website is not intended or implied to be a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis or treatment. All content, including text, graphics, images and information, contained on or available through this web site is for general information purposes only.

  • Healing the Mother Wound
faqHtml:
Frequently Asked Questions

Q: I feel completely touched-out and depleted. Where do I even start?
A: Start with the floor. Seriously. Lie on the floor, on your back, and just feel the support beneath you. Do not try to meditate or fix anything. Your only job is to feel the sensation of contact between your body and the ground. This is the most basic, foundational way to begin signaling safety to your nervous system. Do this for five minutes a day. That is the entire practice. Before you can connect with another, you must reconnect with the earth itself.

Q: My partner is the one who is always checked out. How can I get them to do this work?
A: You can't. Stop trying. Your focus on "fixing" them is a subtle form of aggression and a brilliant way to avoid your own work. The most powerful thing you can do is to commit fiercely to your own practice. Cultivate your own resource. Fill your own well. When you change your own energetic state, the dynamic between you will inevitably shift. Your partner will either begin to resonate with your new level of presence, or the gap between you will become so clear that the next step will be obvious. Do your work. That is all.
